title: Spring Boot Core
content: Reference for dependency injection, bean annotations, lifecycle, scopes, component scanning, conditional annotations, application events, ApplicationContext, and AOP.

sections.title: Spring IoC Container
sections.description: Inversion of Control concepts, container mechanics, BeanDefinition, and container bootstrapping.

blocks.html:
Inversion of Control (IoC) means the framework controls object creation and wiring instead of application code. In traditional code, a class instantiates its own dependencies: new OrderService(new JdbcOrderRepository(new DataSource(...))). With IoC, the container owns that graph — you declare what each class needs; the container builds and connects everything.

Spring's IoC container is the BeanFactory and its extension ApplicationContext. At startup it reads bean definitions from configuration sources (annotations, Java config, XML), resolves the dependency graph, instantiates beans in the correct order, and manages their full lifecycle. Application code receives fully-wired objects.

blocks.title: Traditional vs IoC Object Creation
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram comparing traditional object creation against Spring IoC container-managed creation.

Use `graph TD` layout with two side-by-side subgraphs.

Left subgraph — label: `Traditional (Manual Wiring)`:
- `main()` → creates → `DataSource`
- `main()` → creates → `JdbcOrderRepository(DataSource)`
- `main()` → creates → `PaymentGateway`
- `main()` → creates → `OrderService(JdbcOrderRepository, PaymentGateway)`
- Note: `Application code owns object graph`

Right subgraph — label: `IoC Container`:
- `@Configuration / @Component` → `BeanDefinitions`
- `BeanDefinitions` → read by → `ApplicationContext`
- `ApplicationContext` → resolves dependency graph
- `ApplicationContext` → instantiates → `DataSource`, `OrderRepository`, `PaymentGateway`, `OrderService`
- `ApplicationContext` → injects → dependencies automatically
- Note: `Container owns object graph. Code declares needs.`

Style: monochrome, rectangular nodes, clear subgraph borders.

paragraphs:
• BeanDefinition: Metadata describing a bean — class, scope, init/destroy method names, constructor args, property values. The container builds this from your annotations or config classes before instantiating anything.
  • BeanFactory: The root interface. Provides getBean() and lazy instantiation. Rarely used directly in production code.
  • ApplicationContext: Extends BeanFactory with eager instantiation of singletons, event publishing, i18n, and AOP integration. The container you interact with in Spring Boot.
  • BeanDefinitionRegistry: Where BeanDefinition objects are stored. ApplicationContext implements this. You can register beans programmatically here before context refresh.
  • BeanPostProcessor: Hooks into the instantiation pipeline to wrap or modify beans after construction. AOP proxy creation, @Autowired injection, and @Value resolution all run through BeanPostProcessor implementations.

blocks.title: IoC Container Bootstrapping Flow
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid flowchart showing how the Spring IoC container bootstraps from configuration to a running context.

Use `flowchart TD` layout.

Steps in order:
1. `Configuration Sources` — three parallel input nodes:
   - `@Component-annotated classes` (via classpath scan)
   - `@Configuration + @Bean methods` (explicit declarations)
   - `XML or properties files` (legacy / external config)
2. → `BeanDefinitionReader` parses each source
3. → `BeanDefinition objects` registered in `BeanDefinitionRegistry`
4. → `BeanFactoryPostProcessor` runs (e.g. PropertyPlaceholderConfigurer resolves ${...} placeholders in definitions)
5. → `Dependency graph resolved` — instantiation order determined
6. → For each BeanDefinition (singleton, in order):
   a. `Instantiation` — constructor called
   b. `BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization()`
   c. `Init callbacks` (@PostConstruct, afterPropertiesSet, initMethod)
   d. `BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization()` — AOP proxy created here
7. → `ApplicationContext ready` — all singleton beans in cache
8. → `Application code calls getBean() or uses injected refs` — returns cached singleton

Style: decision-free flowchart, rectangular nodes, monochrome, subgraph for step 6 labeled `Per-Bean Initialization`.

rows.description: Fully-qualified class name of the bean.
Bean scope: singleton, prototype, request, etc.
If true, bean is created on first access rather than at context refresh.
Constructor arguments — types and values resolved before instantiation.
Property-based dependencies injected after construction.
Method to call after initialization. Equivalent to @Bean(initMethod=...).
Method to call on context close.
If true, this bean is preferred over others of the same type when auto-wiring.
Bean names that must be initialized before this one. Enforces ordering without a direct dependency.
If false, excluded from auto-wire resolution by type.

paragraphs:
IoC is the principle; Dependency Injection is the implementation mechanism. IoC says the container controls object creation. DI is how the container supplies dependencies — through constructors, setters, or field injection. Every DI-based framework implements IoC, but IoC can be implemented through other patterns (service locator, factory methods) that are not DI.

In Spring: the IoC container reads BeanDefinitions, determines the dependency graph, and resolves it using DI. When it creates OrderService, it sees that the constructor requires OrderRepository, finds the registered bean of that type, creates or retrieves it, and passes it in. This process recurses until the full graph is satisfied.

rows.use_case: Application beans — domain classes, services, controllers, repositories.
Infrastructure beans — DataSource, RestTemplate, third-party library instances that can't be annotated.
Spring Boot starters — auto-wired infrastructure activated by classpath presence.
Dynamic beans — number or type of beans determined at runtime.
Legacy applications. Not used in modern Spring Boot.

paragraphs:
• Context refresh: The moment all singleton beans are instantiated. Triggered by context.refresh() internally during SpringApplication.run(). After refresh, the context is immutable — no new bean definitions can be added without a restart.
  • Singleton cache: After a singleton bean is instantiated, it is stored in a Map<String, Object> inside DefaultSingletonBeanRegistry. Subsequent getBean() calls return the cached instance — no re-instantiation.
  • Three-level cache: Spring uses three caches during instantiation to resolve circular dependencies for setter/field injection. Level 1: fully initialized singletons. Level 2: early exposed singletons (post-processed). Level 3: singleton factories (raw instances, pre-post-processing). Constructor injection bypasses this mechanism — circular deps fail fast.

sections.title: Dependency Injection
sections.description: DI types, injection styles, trade-offs, and circular dependency handling.

blocks.html: Spring's IoC container manages bean creation and wiring. You declare what a class needs; the container supplies it. Three injection styles exist: constructor, setter, and field. They differ in immutability guarantees, testability, and when nullability errors surface.

blocks.title: DI Types Overview
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing the three Spring dependency injection styles and their key characteristics.

Use `graph TD` layout.

Top node: `Spring IoC Container`
Three branches from the top node:
1. `Constructor Injection`
   - child note: `Dependencies passed via constructor`
   - child note: `Immutable fields, required deps`
   - child note: `Best for testing`
2. `Setter Injection`
   - child note: `Dependencies set via setter methods`
   - child note: `Optional deps, reconfigurable`
3. `Field Injection`
   - child note: `@Autowired on field directly`
   - child note: `No testing without Spring context`
   - child note: `Avoid in production code`

Each branch uses rectangular nodes. Add a warning marker node under `Field Injection` labeled `Not recommended`.

Style: monochrome, no color fills, clean.

blocks.html: Constructor injection passes all dependencies as constructor parameters. The bean is fully initialized by the time it exits the constructor. Spring 4.3+ auto-wires a single constructor without requiring @Autowired.

paragraphs:
• Immutability: Dependencies declared final. Cannot be reassigned after construction. Eliminates accidental state mutation.
  • Null safety at construction time: A missing required bean fails fast — context refresh throws before the application starts. No NullPointerException at runtime.
  • Testability: Instantiate the class directly in tests: new OrderService(mockRepo, mockGateway, mockNotifier). No Spring context required.
  • Explicit contract: All required dependencies visible in one place — the constructor signature. Easy to detect classes with too many dependencies.

blocks.html: Setter injection uses @Autowired on setter methods. Dependencies are injected after the constructor runs. Use for optional dependencies or when a class needs to be reconfigured after construction — both uncommon in typical Spring applications.

Field injection places @Autowired directly on instance fields. Spring uses reflection to inject the value after construction. The field cannot be final, and the class cannot be instantiated outside a Spring context without additional tooling.

blocks.title: Field Injection Drawbacks
blocks.bodyHtml: Not testable without Spring: To unit test, you must either load a Spring context or use ReflectionTestUtils.setField() — both add overhead. Hides dependencies: A class with 10 @Autowired fields has an obvious design problem, but it's easier to ignore than 10 constructor parameters. Cannot be final: Dependencies are mutable after construction, which violates encapsulation. Circular dependency masking: Field injection can hide circular dependencies that constructor injection would detect at startup.

paragraphs:
A circular dependency exists when Bean A requires Bean B and Bean B requires Bean A. With constructor injection, Spring detects this at startup and throws BeanCurrentlyInCreationException. With field or setter injection, Spring resolves it using partially-initialized proxies — the dependency is injected before the bean finishes initialization, which can lead to subtle bugs.

The correct fix is a design change: extract the shared logic into a third bean, or use an event-driven approach to break the dependency. As a last resort, annotate one constructor parameter with @Lazy — Spring injects a proxy and resolves the real bean on first method call. Setting spring.main.allow-circular-references=true (Boot 2.6+ default: false) re-enables the old behavior; avoid this — it treats a symptom, not the cause.

sections.title: Bean Annotations Complete Reference
sections.description: Full attribute reference for component stereotypes, bean declaration, and injection annotations.

blocks.html: Spring's bean annotations fall into three categories: component stereotypes (@Component and its specializations), bean declaration annotations (@Bean, @Configuration), and injection annotations (@Autowired, @Qualifier, @Primary). Stereotype annotations trigger component scanning; @Bean methods register beans explicitly in a configuration class.

rows.description: Bean name. If empty, defaults to the simple class name with the first letter lowercased: MyComponent → myComponent.
Bean name. Semantically identical to @Component. Marks the class as a service-layer component. Triggers exception translation when combined with Spring Data.
Bean name. Activates Spring's persistence exception translation: vendor-specific exceptions (SQLException, Hibernate exceptions) are wrapped in Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy.
Bean name. Marks the class as an MVC controller. Handler methods return view names or model-and-view objects. Use @ResponseBody on individual methods to return raw data.
Bean name. Composed annotation: @Controller + @ResponseBody. All handler methods serialize return values directly to the HTTP response body via HttpMessageConverter.
One or more bean names. First name is the primary; additional names are aliases.
Name of the method to call after the bean is initialized. Alternative to @PostConstruct for third-party classes.
Name of the method to call during context shutdown. Default auto-detects close() or shutdown() methods.
If false, the bean is excluded from auto-wire resolution. Can still be retrieved by name.
Bean name for the configuration class itself.

If true, Spring subclasses the configuration class with CGLIB. Inter-bean @Bean method calls go through the proxy and return the singleton. If false, calls go directly — each call creates a new instance.
If false, allows overloaded @Bean methods. Not recommended — leads to ambiguous bean definitions.
If true, throws NoSuchBeanDefinitionException at startup when no matching bean exists. If false, leaves the dependency null when absent — use for optional dependencies.
Bean name or qualifier label to match against. Resolves ambiguity when multiple beans of the same type exist. Can be placed on both the injection point and the bean definition.
Marks a bean as the preferred candidate when multiple beans of the same type exist and no @Qualifier is specified. Only one bean per type should be @Primary.
Bean name to inject. Resolution order: by name → by type → by qualifier. From jakarta.annotation.Resource.
Bean type to inject. Used when name-based resolution fails.
JNDI lookup name. Rarely used in Spring Boot applications.

Equivalent to @Autowired(required=true). From jakarta.inject.Inject. Requires jakarta.inject:jakarta.inject-api dependency. Combine with @Named (CDI equivalent of @Qualifier) for disambiguation. Prefer @Autowired in Spring-only codebases.

sections.title: Bean Lifecycle
sections.description: Phase sequence, annotation-based callbacks, interface-based callbacks, and initialization order.

blocks.html: Spring manages bean creation through a defined sequence of phases: instantiation, dependency injection, awareness callbacks, initialization callbacks, ready state, and destruction callbacks. Understanding this order is necessary when coordinating initialization logic across multiple beans or integrating with non-Spring lifecycle hooks.

blocks.title: Bean Lifecycle Phases
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid flowchart showing the complete Spring bean lifecycle from instantiation to destruction.

Use `flowchart TD` layout.

Phases in order (top to bottom):
1. `Instantiation` — constructor called
2. `Dependency Injection` — @Autowired fields, setters, constructor args resolved
3. `BeanNameAware.setBeanName()` — if implemented
4. `BeanFactoryAware.setBeanFactory()` — if implemented
5. `ApplicationContextAware.setApplicationContext()` — if implemented
6. `BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization()` — all registered processors
7. `@PostConstruct method` — JSR-250 init callback
8. `InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet()` — if implemented
9. `@Bean(initMethod=...)` — custom init method
10. `BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization()` — all registered processors
11. `Bean is Ready` (in ApplicationContext, serving requests)
12. `@PreDestroy method` — on context close
13. `DisposableBean.destroy()` — if implemented
14. `@Bean(destroyMethod=...)` — custom destroy method

Connect each phase with downward arrows.
Group phases 6-10 in a subgraph labeled `Initialization Phase`.
Group phases 12-14 in a subgraph labeled `Destruction Phase`.

Style: monochrome, no color fills, rectangular nodes.

rows.notes: Dependencies resolved first for constructor injection.
All dependencies injected before any callback runs.
All registered BeanPostProcessor beans run in order. AOP proxy creation happens here.
Three mechanisms run in this fixed order if all are present.
Final opportunity to wrap the bean — e.g. return a proxy.
Bean is available for injection and method calls.
Triggered by context.close() or JVM shutdown hook.

rows.description: Marks a method to invoke after dependency injection completes and before the bean is put into service. Must be void, take no parameters, and not throw checked exceptions. From jakarta.annotation.PostConstruct. Runs before InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet().

Marks a method to invoke before the bean is removed from the context during shutdown. Must be void, take no parameters. Use to release resources: connections, thread pools, file handles, scheduled tasks. From jakarta.annotation.PreDestroy. Not called for prototype-scoped beans.

paragraphs: Implement InitializingBean and override afterPropertiesSet() to run initialization logic after all properties are set. Runs after @PostConstruct methods, before the custom initMethod defined in @Bean. Prefer @PostConstruct for new code — InitializingBean couples the class to Spring's API.

Implement DisposableBean and override destroy() to run cleanup logic before the bean is removed. Runs after @PreDestroy methods, before the custom destroyMethod. Same trade-off as InitializingBean: couples the class to Spring. Prefer @PreDestroy or @Bean(destroyMethod=...) for third-party classes.

sections.title: Bean Scopes
sections.description: Available scopes, @Scope attributes, and scope proxy mode.

blocks.html: Bean scope determines how many instances Spring creates and how long they live. Singleton is the default — one instance per ApplicationContext. Web-aware scopes (request, session, application) require a web-capable context. Prototype creates a new instance on every injection point or getBean() call — Spring does not manage its destruction.

rows.description: Scope name. Built-in values: singleton, prototype, request, session, application, websocket.
Required when injecting a short-lived scoped bean into a singleton. TARGET_CLASS uses CGLIB proxy; INTERFACES uses JDK dynamic proxy.

sections.title: Component Scanning
sections.description: @ComponentScan configuration, include/exclude filters, and lazy initialization.

blocks.html: Component scanning discovers beans by inspecting the classpath for classes annotated with @Component and its specializations. @SpringBootApplication activates scanning from the main class's package downward. Configure @ComponentScan explicitly when the main class is not at the root package, or when scanning must include or exclude specific classes.

blocks.title: Component Scanning Process
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid flowchart showing how Spring component scanning discovers and registers beans.

Use `flowchart TD` layout.

Steps:
1. `@ComponentScan activated` (by @SpringBootApplication or explicit @ComponentScan)
2. → `ClassPathScanningCandidateComponentProvider` scans specified base packages
3. → For each class found:
   - Check: `Is annotated with @Component or a stereotype?`
   - Yes → Check `Include/Exclude filters`
     - Passes filters → `Register bean definition` in BeanDefinitionRegistry
     - Fails filters → `Skip class`
   - No → `Skip class`
4. → `BeanDefinition registered` → available for dependency resolution

Add a side note: `@Filter(ANNOTATION, value=...)` and `@Filter(ASSIGNABLE_TYPE, ...)` etc. branch off the filter step.

Style: decision diamonds for checks, rectangles for actions, monochrome.

rows.description: Root packages to scan. All sub-packages are included recursively.
Type-safe alternative. Spring scans the package of each specified class. Use a marker interface or class for each module.
Additional filter rules to include classes not matching default stereotype detection. Requires useDefaultFilters=false to be meaningful as an addition.
Filter rules to exclude matching classes from registration. Applied after include filters.
If false, disables default detection of @Component and stereotypes. Combine with includeFilters for precise control.
If true, all scanned beans are initialized lazily — on first access rather than at startup.
Strategy for generating bean names from scanned class names.

rows.matches: Classes annotated with the specified annotation. Most common filter type.
Classes that are assignable to the specified class or interface.
Classes matching an AspectJ type pattern expression.
Classes whose fully-qualified name matches a regex pattern.
Classes passing a custom TypeFilter implementation. Receives class metadata for arbitrary logic.

sections.title: Conditional Annotations
sections.description: Full reference for all @ConditionalOn* annotations with attributes, examples, and custom conditions.

blocks.html: @Conditional annotations control whether a @Bean or @Configuration class is registered based on runtime conditions evaluated during context refresh. They are the mechanism behind Spring Boot's auto-configuration — each auto-config class is guarded by one or more conditions that check classpath, existing beans, or property values.

rows.activates_when: Specified class(es) present on the classpath
Specified class(es) absent from the classpath
Specified bean(s) already exist in the context
Specified bean(s) not yet registered in the context
Property present and matches expected value
Classpath resource exists at specified path
Running in a web application context
Not running in a web application context
JVM is a specific Java version or range
Exactly one bean of specified type exists (or one is @Primary)

rows.description: Classes that must be present. Safe to reference — if absent, the condition simply fails rather than causing a ClassNotFoundException.
Fully-qualified class names. Use when the class may not be on the compile classpath at all.
Bean types that must already exist in the context.
Bean names that must already exist.
Beans annotated with the specified annotation must exist.
ALL (entire hierarchy), CURRENT (current context only), ANCESTORS (parent contexts only).
Property key prefix. Combined with name: prefix.name.
Property key(s). All must match for the condition to pass.
Expected property value. Empty string means any non-false value passes.
If true, condition passes when the property is absent. Useful for enabling features by default that can be explicitly disabled.

paragraphs:
@ConditionalOnMissingBean and @ConditionalOnBean depend on what beans are already registered at the point of evaluation. Bean definition order matters: if Bean A's condition checks for Bean B, Bean B's configuration class must be processed first. Control order with @AutoConfigureBefore / @AutoConfigureAfter for auto-configurations, or @DependsOn for manual ordering.

Conditions are evaluated per-bean-definition during context refresh, not per-class. A single @Configuration class can have multiple @Bean methods each with different @Conditional annotations evaluated independently.

sections.title: Application Events
sections.description: Built-in events, @EventListener, custom events, publishing, async, ordered, and transactional events.

blocks.html: Spring's event system provides decoupled communication between beans. A publisher fires an event; registered listeners receive it. Events are synchronous by default — the publisher blocks until all listeners complete. Use @Async on listeners for non-blocking event handling, or @TransactionalEventListener to bind listener execution to transaction phases.

blocks.title: Event Publishing Flow
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid sequence diagram showing Spring application event publishing and listening.

Participants:
- `Publisher Bean`
- `ApplicationEventPublisher`
- `SimpleApplicationEventMulticaster`
- `Listener A (@EventListener)`
- `Listener B (@Async @EventListener)`
- `Listener C (@TransactionalEventListener)`

Flow:
1. `Publisher Bean` → `ApplicationEventPublisher.publishEvent(OrderPlacedEvent)`
2. `ApplicationEventPublisher` → `SimpleApplicationEventMulticaster.multicastEvent()`
3. `SimpleApplicationEventMulticaster` → `Listener A` (synchronous, blocks publisher)
4. `SimpleApplicationEventMulticaster` → `Listener B` (async, separate thread via TaskExecutor)
5. `SimpleApplicationEventMulticaster` → `Listener C` (deferred until AFTER_COMMIT of current transaction)
6. `Listener A` → returns → `Publisher Bean` continues

Add note: `@Ordered or @Order determines sequence when multiple sync listeners`

Style: standard Mermaid sequence diagram, monochrome.

rows.fired_when: Application starts, before environment or context setup
Environment ready, context not yet created
Context created, initializers called, beans not loaded
Bean definitions loaded, context not refreshed
Context fully refreshed — all beans instantiated
Runners complete, application ready for traffic

rows.description: Event types to listen for. If empty, inferred from the method's parameter type.
SpEL expression. Listener only invoked when expression evaluates to true. Access event via #root.event or #event.
Identifier for the listener. Used for targeted event reply via ReplyEventPublisher.

sections.title: ApplicationContext
sections.description: Context types, key methods, BeanFactory differences, and Aware interfaces.

blocks.html: ApplicationContext is Spring's central interface for accessing the IoC container. It extends BeanFactory with enterprise features: event publishing, internationalization, resource loading, and application lifecycle management. Direct access to the context in application code is a code smell — prefer injecting specific beans. Access the context only when the bean type or name is not known at compile time.

blocks.title: ApplicationContext Type Hierarchy
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid class diagram showing the ApplicationContext interface hierarchy.

Use `classDiagram` layout.

Hierarchy (from most general to most specific):
- `BeanFactory` (interface) → extended by `ApplicationContext`
- `ApplicationContext` (interface) → extended by:
  - `ConfigurableApplicationContext` (interface)
    - implemented by `AbstractApplicationContext`
      - extended by `AnnotationConfigApplicationContext` (non-web)
      - extended by `GenericWebApplicationContext`
        - extended by `AnnotationConfigServletWebServerApplicationContext` (Boot servlet)
        - extended by `AnnotationConfigReactiveWebServerApplicationContext` (Boot reactive)

Also show:
- `ApplicationContext` → implemented by `ClassPathXmlApplicationContext` (legacy)
- `ApplicationContext` extends `MessageSource`, `ApplicationEventPublisher`, `ResourcePatternResolver`

Label each concrete class with a brief note:
- `AnnotationConfigApplicationContext`: "Used in non-web apps and tests"
- `AnnotationConfigServletWebServerApplicationContext`: "Default in Spring Boot servlet stack"
- `AnnotationConfigReactiveWebServerApplicationContext`: "Default in Spring Boot WebFlux"

Style: standard Mermaid classDiagram notation, monochrome.

rows.use_case: Standalone apps, integration tests, non-web contexts. Load from @Configuration classes.
Default context for Spring Boot servlet (Tomcat/Jetty/Undertow) apps.
Default context for Spring Boot WebFlux (Reactor Netty) apps.
Legacy XML-based configuration. Rarely used in Boot applications.
Base class for web contexts. Used in testing with MockMvc.

rows.description: Retrieves a bean by type. Throws if 0 or more than 1 match.
Retrieves a bean by name and type.
All beans of the given type: key=name, value=instance.
Names of all beans assignable to the given type.
True if a bean with the given name is registered.
True if the named bean is singleton-scoped.
Access to the unified property/profile abstraction.
Publish an application event to all registered listeners.
Load a resource by location string (classpath:, file:, http:).
Unique identifier for this context. Useful when multiple contexts exist.

rows.provides: The full ApplicationContext. Prefer constructor injection of specific beans.
The name under which this bean is registered in the context.
Environment for property and profile access. Prefer @Value or @ConfigurationProperties.
ResourceLoader for classpath and file resource access.

sections.title: AOP — Aspect Oriented Programming
sections.description: AOP concepts, advice annotations with attributes, pointcut expressions, proxy types, and ordering.

blocks.html:
AOP separates cross-cutting concerns — logging, security, transaction management, caching — from business logic. Spring AOP uses proxy-based weaving: at runtime, Spring wraps the target bean in a proxy that intercepts method calls matching defined pointcuts and executes advice code around them.

Spring AOP applies only to Spring-managed beans and only to public method calls through the proxy. Direct method calls within the same class bypass the proxy and skip all advice. For intra-class method interception, use AspectJ load-time or compile-time weaving.

blocks.title: AOP Concepts
blocks.description:
PROMPT FOR DIAGRAM GENERATION:

Generate a Mermaid diagram showing AOP concepts and their relationships.

Use `graph LR` (left-to-right) layout.

Nodes and relationships:
- `Aspect` — contains → `Pointcut` and `Advice`
- `Pointcut` — matches → `JoinPoint` (method execution on a Spring bean)
- `Advice` — runs at → `JoinPoint` (when Pointcut matches)
- `Advice` types (sub-nodes of Advice):
  - `@Before` — before method execution
  - `@After` — after method execution (always)
  - `@AfterReturning` — after method returns normally
  - `@AfterThrowing` — after method throws exception
  - `@Around` — wraps the method, controls execution
- `Weaving` — creates → `AOP Proxy`
- `AOP Proxy` — wraps → `Target Bean`
- `Caller` → calls `AOP Proxy` → delegates to `Target Bean`

Style: monochrome, rectangular nodes for concepts, clear directional arrows with labels.

rows.definition: A class annotated with @Aspect that encapsulates a cross-cutting concern. Contains pointcuts and advice.
A point in program execution where advice can be applied. In Spring AOP, always a method execution on a Spring-managed bean.
An expression that matches a set of JoinPoints. Defined with AspectJ expression language. Advice is bound to a pointcut.
Code executed at a JoinPoint. Types: Before, After, AfterReturning, AfterThrowing, Around.
Linking aspects to target beans. Spring AOP weaves at runtime using proxies. AspectJ weaves at compile time or load time.
The original bean being proxied. Advice is applied around calls to the target's methods.
Declaring additional interfaces on a proxied bean without modifying the target class. Implemented via @DeclareParents.

rows.description: Marks a class as an AspectJ aspect. Requires @EnableAspectJAutoProxy on a configuration class (auto-applied by Spring Boot when spring-boot-starter-aop is on the classpath). The class must also be a Spring bean — annotate with @Component or declare with @Bean.
Pointcut expression or named pointcut reference. Executes before the matched method. Cannot prevent method execution (use @Around for that).
Comma-separated parameter names when debug info is unavailable and parameters must be bound by name.
Runs after the method exits — normal or exception. Analogous to finally. Cannot access return value or exception.
Runs only on normal return. Bind return value via returning — method parameter must match the declared type.
Runs only when the method throws. Bind the exception via throwing. Cannot suppress the exception — use @Around to catch and swallow.
Pointcut expression. The advice method must accept ProceedingJoinPoint as its first parameter.
Comma-separated parameter names. Needed when debug information is stripped and parameters are bound by name.
AspectJ pointcut expression. The annotated method must have an empty body and void return type. Other advice methods reference the pointcut by method name.

rows.matches: All methods within a type or package.
Methods annotated with the specified annotation.
All methods in types annotated with specified annotation.
Methods with arguments of specified types.
Methods whose arguments are annotated with specified annotation.
Spring bean name or name pattern.
Target object is an instance of specified type.

paragraphs:
• JDK dynamic proxy: Default when the target implements at least one interface. Creates a proxy implementing the same interfaces. The injected bean is an interface type — cast to the concrete class fails.
  • CGLIB proxy: Used when the target does not implement any interface, or when proxyTargetClass=true is set. Subclasses the target class. Cannot proxy final classes or final methods. CGLIB is also used when @Configuration(proxyBeanMethods=true).
  • Self-invocation limitation: Calling a proxied method from within the same class bypasses the proxy — no advice runs. Solutions: inject the bean into itself via @Autowired @Lazy private MyService self, use AopContext.currentProxy(), or restructure to a separate bean.

rows.description: If true, forces CGLIB proxies for all beans, even those implementing interfaces. Spring Boot sets this to true by default.
If true, the current proxy is exposed via AopContext.currentProxy(). Required for self-invocation workaround. Has a thread-local overhead cost.
